Do you have FoC-related interests, like making programming languages or whatever?

elements.text: Do you have FoC-related interests, like making programming languages or whatever?

text: That’s a good question! What counts? I have no immediate plans to revolutionize the world with a great new scottlang.. but understanding/improving and experimenting with how people and computers can work well together has always been interesting. I’m hoping to catch up on the FoC content (podcast and web page and links) and then might be better able to answer your question :)

I would actually argue that making programming languages is not an essential FoC-related interest. Which is why I've campaigned in the past to push discussions of programming language minutiae out of <#C5T9GPWFL|general> and into <#C5U3SEW6A|random>. There are other communities to discuss programming languages; for the most part programming languages seem about the present of computing rather than the future, perpetuating the status quo rather than interrogating it.

text:
<@UC2A2ARPT> I actually spent a while trying to articulate that in my previous comment before backspacing over it :slightly_smiling_face: Ok, if nudged I'll type it out again.

I'd say the idealization of FoC is that we're trying to make programming-related skills more accessible to new people with diverse expertise. Whether that's bringing programming techniques to non-programmers, or bringing compiler skills to Rails programmers.

In practice, building new prototypes takes so long that we have trouble getting out of our heads and talking to real users.

text:
That's nice. It doesn't encompass projects that are trying to make programming more powerful for expert programmers, though, which is the camp I think I fall into.

Because it's fun to play this game, here's my crack in the nut: we're a community looking for any boundaries and limits of current programming practice — ease, capability, inclusion, visibility, dynamicity, comprehensibility, physicality, whatever — trying to study and imagine what's beyond those bounds, and trying to build it.

This game is hard.

text:
I'm in the same camp as you! I think it fits my definition, in a "this quadratic equation has two roots but they are equal" kind of way. Making programming more powerful can be recast as making some bleeding-edge technique "more accessible" (as in possible at all).

Maybe I've now made the definition so vapid as to include programming languages :smile: Perhaps the dividing line is: even if it's for experts now, do you consider providing it to non-experts part of _your_ goal, or have you wrapped that side in a "somebody else's problem" field? For me, supporting experts is just a sub-goal.

text: <@UC2A2ARPT> and <@UCUSW7WVD> is there a reference definition of ‘programming’ for your definitions? I don’t want to get stuck in the details, but I’m curious. What makes something programming instead of say, building an interactive spreadsheet, or building an interactive prototype in powerpoint? Are we mostly interested in what we currently do as text entry with a ‘programming’ language? But I hear lots of interest in visual/patch programming tools. To me all of this definitely falls under human-computer interaction, but is it a well defined subset of that?

text:
I definitely don't intend to exclude spreadsheets. Powerpoint prototypes I haven't heard of. Do you mean like slightly interactive mockups?

I think programming activities lie on a spectrum based on how much thought they require for testing (making one's intent precise, and encoding this precise intent) and design (being conscious of assumptions and trade-offs, and encoding them so they can be revisited). Neither attribute intrinsically privileges text mode, even if all the ways we currently scale along these axes currently rely on text.

Here's how I think spreadsheets do against this standard: <https://futureofcoding.slack.com/archives/CLYCGTCPL/p1572456721136600?thread_ts=1572002424.061800&cid=CLYCGTCPL> (For however long the link works.)

attachments.fallback:
[October 30th, 2019 10:32 AM] ak: Hmm, not sure I understand that distinction.

Perhaps a concrete example will help, in two parts:
1. Spreadsheets are great for non-programmers. Let's generalize spreadsheets beyond tables.
2. Spreadsheets often grow too complex to debug, and bugs are often missed. Let's improve spreadsheets to be easier to debug.

I've long suspected both these endeavors to be doomed. If you focus on just one side the other will get you. And if you try to focus on both the problem ends up getting too constrained.

The issue here is that both problems are Romanticist in their framing. Here's a Classicist reframing: spreadsheets are great for non-programmers but they're _fundamentally incomplete_. Let's use spreadsheets as a gateway drug to teach people to test what they build, value simple solutions over complex ones, decouple solutions into sub-systems, etc.

I'm starting to think Alan Kay and Bret Victor would agree with this. The new steelman model of them in my head agrees that tools need to help people change, but just hasn't found a way to clearly communicate this nuance at scale. Their fundamental goal is to inspire.

[November 8th, 2019 5:57 PM] dcook0819: What counts as "code" or "programming"?

This was an interesting topic between <@U5TCAFTD3>  and <@U6BRN7A0Y> in podcast #43 (Unveiling Dark). Some of the contested areas were:
* Incidental (accidental) complexity, vs essential "this is what it does" code
* Whether it looks like "code" / is symbolic
* Whether it's the boring / tedius stuff, or the "fun" part of programming

What does everyone else think?

text: +1 Ivan... programming needs to be more accessible to BOTH beginners and experts. I've been programming over 20 years, and it's not unusual that I'd like to automate something but I don't bother because I don't know how. Nor is it unusual that I run into a problem that I have no way to diagnose (e.g. on the weekend something kept writing to my HDD until it was full, but I had no way to figure out which process was doing it or what file they were writing to), or I see a program with an unusual feature and I wonder "how did they do that?" but I have no way to find out... It seems to me that most of the things people are trying to do here are valuable, whether it's improvements to traditional programming languages, code visualization, debugging tools, figuring out how to make other people's software easier to tweak, etc.
